GG.description: Very high likelihood of brown eyes (>95%)

AG.description: Intermediate pigmentation, likely green or hazel eyes (70-80%)

AA.description: Very high likelihood of blue eyes (>90%)

description: Primary determinant of eye color variation in Europeans. This SNP in the HERC2 gene regulates OCA2 expression, controlling melanin production in the iris.

sources.name: Sturm RA et al. (2008) - A single SNP in an evolutionary conserved region within intron 86 of the HERC2 gene determines human blue-brown eye color

notes: Strongest single genetic predictor of eye color in Europeans, explaining ~75% of blue vs brown variation. Effect is weaker in non-European populations where brown eyes are nearly universal. Works in concert with OCA2 and other pigmentation loci.

CC.description: Normal melanin production, contributes to darker eye color

CT.description: Slightly reduced melanin, may lighten eye color

TT.description: Reduced melanin production, contributes to lighter eye colors

description: OCA2 gene variant that modifies eye color by affecting melanin production. Works in combination with rs12913832.

sources.name: Duffy DL et al. (2007) - A three-single-nucleotide polymorphism haplotype in intron 1 of OCA2 explains most human eye-color variation

notes: Modifier of eye color that works alongside rs12913832 (HERC2). Most impactful in individuals with intermediate pigmentation — can shift hazel eyes toward blue or green. Effect size is moderate compared to the primary HERC2 variant.

GG.description: Higher melanin, contributes to darker eye colors

TT.description: Lower melanin, contributes to lighter eye colors (especially green)

description: SLC24A4 gene variant associated with green eye color variation. Secondary modifier of eye pigmentation.

sources.name: Sulem et al. (2008) - Genetic determinants of hair, eye and skin pigmentation in Europeans

notes: Contributing locus for eye color identified in European GWAS. Has a smaller effect than HERC2/OCA2 variants but adds predictive power in multi-SNP models. Also weakly associated with hair color variation.

CC.description: Normal MC1R function, no red hair from this variant

CT.description: Carrier of red hair variant, may have subtle red tints or increased freckling

TT.description: High likelihood of red or strawberry blonde hair (80-90%)

description: Most common MC1R variant associated with red hair. Loss of function mutation in melanocortin receptor, shifting eumelanin to pheomelanin production.

sources.name: Valverde P et al. (1995) - Variants of the melanocyte-stimulating hormone receptor gene are associated with red hair and fair skin in humans

notes: One of the strongest MC1R red hair variants (R151C). Highly penetrant in homozygotes — ~80% chance of red hair. Also increases melanoma risk independently of hair color. Most common in Northern European populations (Ireland, Scotland).

CC.description: Normal MC1R function

CT.description: Carrier status, possible subtle red tones

TT.description: High likelihood of red or auburn hair (75-85%)

description: Another common MC1R red hair variant (R160W mutation). Often found in combination with other MC1R variants.

sources.name: Valverde P et al. (1995) - Variants of the melanocyte-stimulating hormone receptor gene are associated with red hair and fair skin in humans

notes: MC1R variant (R160W) associated with red hair, though less penetrant than R151C. Compound heterozygosity with other MC1R variants increases red hair probability. Also linked to increased sun sensitivity and freckling.

CC.description: Higher melanin production, darker hair color

CT.description: Intermediate melanin, light brown or dark blond hair

TT.description: Reduced melanin, increased likelihood of blond hair in Europeans

description: KITLG gene variant strongly associated with blond hair in European populations. Each T allele increases blond hair likelihood.

sources.name: Sulem et al. (2008) - Genetic determinants of hair, eye and skin pigmentation in Europeans

notes: Associated with blond hair in Europeans via regulation of KITLG expression in hair follicles. Effect is population-specific — rare outside European-descent populations. Explains only a fraction of blond hair variation; many other loci contribute.

CC.description: Normal melanin production, darker hair and skin

CT.description: Intermediate pigmentation levels

TT.description: Reduced melanin, lighter hair (especially blond/red) and skin, more freckling

description: IRF4 transcription factor variant affecting melanocyte function. Associated with lighter hair, skin, and increased freckling.

sources.name: Han J et al. (2008) - A genome-wide association study identifies novel alleles associated with hair color and skin pigmentation

notes: IRF4 variant with pleiotropic effects on skin, hair, and eye pigmentation. Strongly associated with freckling, sun sensitivity, and lighter hair color in Europeans. Also linked to melanoma susceptibility independently of pigmentation.

TT.description: Typical European/African hair characteristics, thinner hair strands

CT.description: Thicker hair strands, straighter hair

CC.description: Very thick hair strands, very straight hair (common in East Asians)

description: EDAR gene variant (370A allele) prevalent in East Asian populations. Affects hair follicle development, leading to thicker, straighter hair shafts.

sources.name: Fujimoto A et al. (2008) - A scan for genetic determinants of human hair morphology: EDAR is associated with Asian hair thickness

notes: EDAR V370A variant at near-fixation in East Asian and Native American populations but rare elsewhere. Affects hair thickness, tooth morphology, sweat gland density, and breast tissue. One of the strongest examples of recent positive selection in humans.

AA.description: Reduced melanin production, light skin pigmentation (European-type)

AG.description: Intermediate melanin levels, medium skin tone

GG.description: Normal melanin production, darker skin pigmentation

description: Major determinant of light skin in European and South Asian populations. The A allele (derived) reduces melanin ~30-40%.

sources.name: Lamason RL et al. (2005) - SLC24A5, a putative cation exchanger, affects pigmentation in zebrafish and humans

notes: Major determinant of light skin in Europeans and South Asians. The derived A allele is nearly fixed in European populations and at high frequency in South Asia. One of the strongest known examples of convergent evolution for light skin pigmentation.

GG.description: Reduced melanin, lighter skin and eyes (European-type)

CC.description: Normal melanin production, darker skin and eyes

description: SLC45A2 variant affecting melanin synthesis. The G allele is nearly fixed in Europeans and contributes to light skin and eyes.

sources.name: Graf J et al. (2005) - Single nucleotide polymorphisms in the MATP gene are associated with normal human pigmentation variation

notes: SLC45A2 variant strongly associated with light skin and eye color in Europeans. Nearly fixed for the derived allele in Europeans but rare in African and East Asian populations. Has a stronger effect on skin color than eye color.

GG.description: Normal MC1R function, lower freckling tendency

GT.description: Increased freckling tendency, especially with sun exposure

TT.description: Strong freckling tendency, many freckles likely

description: MC1R variant (D84E) strongly associated with freckling, even in non-redheads. Also associated with fair skin and sun sensitivity.

sources.name: Bastiaens M et al. (2001) - Association of the melanocortin-1 receptor gene with freckling and solar lentigines in children

notes: MC1R variant (V60L) associated with freckling and fair skin but rarely causes red hair alone. One of the milder MC1R variants. Freckling tendency is polygenic — MC1R explains the largest fraction but sun exposure is a major environmental modifier.

TT.description: Each T allele adds ~0.4cm to height on average

CT.description: Intermediate height contribution

CC.description: Neutral or slightly reduced height contribution

description: HMGA2 gene variant, one of the strongest single-SNP associations with adult height. However, height is polygenic with 700+ variants.

sources.name: Weedon MN et al. (2007) - A common variant of HMGA2 is associated with adult and childhood height in the general population

notes: HMGA2 variant associated with height variation, though effect size is small (~0.3-0.4 cm per allele). Height is extremely polygenic (thousands of variants). This is one of the more replicated individual loci but explains <0.1% of height variation.

AA.description: Minor positive contribution to height (~0.3cm per allele)

AG.description: Intermediate height contribution

GG.description: Neutral or slightly reduced height contribution

description: GDF5 growth factor variant associated with height and osteoarthritis. Small effect size as height is highly polygenic.

sources.name: Sanna S et al. (2008) - Common variants in the GDF5-UQCC region are associated with variation in human height

notes: GDF5 variant associated with height and osteoarthritis risk. Small effect on height (~0.2 cm per allele). Also implicated in joint development and skeletal morphology. Height prediction requires hundreds of SNPs for meaningful accuracy.

CC.description: Can taste PTC/PROP bitter compounds strongly (supertaster possible)

CG.description: Can taste PTC/PROP bitter compounds (medium sensitivity)

GG.description: Cannot taste PTC/PROP bitter compounds or very weakly

description: TAS2R38 bitter taste receptor variant (PAV/AVI haplotype). Determines ability to taste phenylthiocarbamide (PTC) and PROP bitterness.

sources.name: Kim UK et al. (2003) - Positional cloning of the human quantitative trait locus underlying taste sensitivity to phenylthiocarbamide

notes: One of three TAS2R38 SNPs forming the PAV/AVI haplotype that determines PTC/PROP bitter taste sensitivity. PAV homozygotes are 'supertasters,' AVI homozygotes are non-tasters. The trait follows near-Mendelian inheritance with ~75% accuracy.

TT.description: Reduced bitter taste sensitivity (part of AVI haplotype)

CT.description: Intermediate bitter taste sensitivity

CC.description: Enhanced bitter taste sensitivity (part of PAV haplotype)

description: Another TAS2R38 variant that combines with rs713598 to form PAV/AVI haplotypes determining bitter taste sensitivity.

sources.name: Kim UK et al. (2003) - Positional cloning of the human quantitative trait locus underlying taste sensitivity to phenylthiocarbamide

notes: Part of the TAS2R38 haplotype for bitter taste perception. Works in combination with rs713598 and rs10246939. The three SNPs together determine taster/non-taster status with high accuracy across all studied populations.

TT.description: Enhanced bitter taste sensitivity (part of PAV haplotype)

CT.description: Intermediate bitter taste sensitivity

CC.description: Reduced bitter taste sensitivity (part of AVI haplotype)

description: Third key TAS2R38 variant completing the PAV/AVI haplotype. Works with rs713598 and rs1726866.

sources.name: Kim UK et al. (2003) - Positional cloning of the human quantitative trait locus underlying taste sensitivity to phenylthiocarbamide

notes: Third SNP in the TAS2R38 bitter taste haplotype. The combined PAV/AVI haplotype is one of the best-characterized taste genetics examples. Non-taster frequency varies by population: ~30% in Europeans, ~3% in sub-Saharan Africans.

CC.description: Lactase production stops after childhood, high likelihood of lactose intolerance (>90%)

CT.description: Lactase persistence, can digest lactose in adulthood

TT.description: Strong lactase persistence, very likely can digest lactose throughout life

description: MCM6 regulatory variant controlling LCT (lactase) gene expression. The -13910 C>T variant enables lactase persistence into adulthood in ~35% of world population, mostly Europeans and some African/Middle Eastern groups.

sources.name: Enattah NS et al. (2002) - Identification of a variant associated with adult-type hypolactasia

notes: The primary lactase persistence variant in European populations (-13910C>T). T allele allows continued milk digestion into adulthood. Different lactase persistence mutations exist in African and Middle Eastern populations. One of the best examples of recent human adaptation.

GG.description: Normal ALDH2 enzyme function, no flush reaction

GA.description: Reduced ALDH2 activity, facial flushing with alcohol, increased hangover, higher cancer risk

AA.description: Very low ALDH2 activity, severe facial flushing, nausea with small amounts of alcohol, strongly protective against alcoholism

description: ALDH2*2 variant (Glu504Lys) causing aldehyde dehydrogenase deficiency. Common in East Asians (~40% carriers). Leads to acetaldehyde accumulation and 'Asian flush' reaction.

sources.name: Yoshida A et al. (1984) - Molecular abnormality of an inactive aldehyde dehydrogenase variant commonly found in Orientals

notes: ALDH2*2 variant causing the 'Asian flush' reaction to alcohol. Nearly 40% of East Asians carry at least one copy. Homozygotes have severe alcohol intolerance. Also associated with increased esophageal cancer risk with alcohol consumption.

CC.description: Wet, sticky earwax (European/African type), active apocrine glands, more body odor

CT.description: Wet earwax (dominant), normal body odor

TT.description: Dry, flaky earwax (East Asian type), reduced apocrine gland activity, less body odor

description: ABCC11 variant determining earwax type and apocrine gland secretion. TT genotype (dry earwax) is nearly fixed in East Asians (80-95%) but rare elsewhere.

sources.name: Yoshiura K et al. (2006) - A SNP in the ABCC11 gene is the determinant of human earwax type

notes: Single SNP determining wet vs dry earwax type. The dry earwax allele (T) is near-fixation in East Asian populations. Also determines axillary (underarm) body odor intensity — dry earwax carriers produce less odorous secretions. Classic example of pleiotropic genetic variation.

GG.description: Higher COMT activity, faster dopamine breakdown (Val/Val). Better stress resilience, lower baseline cognition, less pain sensitive

GA.description: Intermediate dopamine metabolism (Val/Met), balanced cognitive/emotional profile

AA.description: Lower COMT activity, slower dopamine breakdown (Met/Met). Better memory and attention, more anxiety-prone, pain sensitive

description: COMT Val158Met variant affecting dopamine metabolism in prefrontal cortex. 'Warrior' (Val) vs 'Worrier' (Met) cognitive-emotional tradeoff.

sources.name: Lachman HM et al. (1996) - Human catechol-O-methyltransferase pharmacogenetics: description of a functional polymorphism and its potential application to neuropsychiatric disorders

notes: COMT Val158Met polymorphism — one of the most studied functional variants in psychiatric genetics. Val allele ('Warrior') has higher enzyme activity and lower prefrontal dopamine; Met allele ('Worrier') has lower activity and higher dopamine. Effects on cognition are context-dependent and modest.

AA.description: Contributes to darker eye pigmentation

AG.description: Moderate eye pigmentation effect

GG.description: Contributes to lighter eye colors (blue/green)

description: Additional OCA2 region variant modifying eye color. Secondary modifier with smaller effect than rs12913832.

sources.name: Duffy DL et al. (2007) - A three-single-nucleotide polymorphism haplotype in intron 1 of OCA2 explains most human eye-color variation

notes: Part of a three-SNP OCA2 haplotype associated with blue/brown eye color. Most studied in European-descent populations. Works additively with HERC2 rs12913832 to fine-tune iris pigmentation.

AA.description: Normal opioid receptor, higher baseline pain sensitivity, better response to opioid medications

AG.description: Intermediate pain sensitivity and opioid response

GG.description: Altered opioid receptor (Asn40Asp), reduced pain sensitivity, may need higher opioid doses

description: OPRM1 mu-opioid receptor variant (A118G) affecting pain perception and opioid medication response. G allele reduces receptor expression.

sources.name: Fillingim RB et al. (2005) - The A118G single nucleotide polymorphism of the mu-opioid receptor gene (OPRM1) is associated with pressure pain sensitivity in humans

notes: OPRM1 A118G variant affecting mu-opioid receptor function. Associated with altered pain sensitivity and variable analgesic response to opioids. Clinical relevance for pain management is being studied but is not yet standard of care. Effect varies by ancestry.

AA.description: Reduced oxytocin sensitivity, less empathetic behavior, more stress reactivity

AG.description: Intermediate oxytocin signaling and social behavior

GG.description: Enhanced oxytocin sensitivity, more empathetic, better social skills, lower stress

description: Oxytocin receptor variant associated with empathy, social behavior, and stress resilience. GG genotype linked to more prosocial behavior.

sources.name: Rodrigues SM et al. (2009) - Oxytocin receptor genetic variation relates to empathy and stress reactivity in humans

notes: OXTR variant widely studied for associations with empathy, social behavior, and stress reactivity. Effect sizes are small and findings have been inconsistent in replication attempts. Social behavior is overwhelmingly shaped by environment; this SNP has minimal predictive value for any individual.

AA.description: Increased androgen receptor sensitivity, higher risk of male pattern baldness

AG.description: Intermediate risk (only in females, males have one copy on X chromosome)

GG.description: Reduced androgen receptor sensitivity, lower baldness risk

description: Androgen receptor gene variant on X chromosome. Males inherit from mother. Affects sensitivity to DHT, primary driver of male pattern baldness.

sources.name: Hillmer AM et al. (2005) - Genetic variation in the human androgen receptor gene is the major determinant of common early-onset androgenetic alopecia

notes: Androgen receptor variant associated with male pattern baldness risk. One of multiple contributing loci — androgenetic alopecia is polygenic. The X-linked pattern means maternal family history is more informative. Environmental factors (stress, nutrition) also play a role.

CC.description: Normal D2 receptor density, less impulsive, lower addiction risk

CT.description: Intermediate dopamine signaling and behavioral traits

TT.description: Reduced D2 receptor density (Taq1A), more novelty seeking, impulsivity, addiction susceptibility

description: ANKK1 variant (formerly called DRD2 Taq1A) affecting dopamine D2 receptor expression. T allele associated with ~30% reduction in receptor density.

sources.name: Noble EP et al. (1998) - D2 dopamine receptor polymorphism and brain regional glucose metabolism

notes: TaqIA polymorphism near DRD2, associated with dopamine receptor density. Originally linked to novelty seeking and addiction risk, but meta-analyses show small and inconsistent effects. Behavioral genetics effects are highly polygenic — single-SNP predictions are unreliable.

GG.description: Normal MC1R function, typical sun sensitivity for skin type

GA.description: Partial MC1R dysfunction, burns more easily, higher melanoma risk

AA.description: Reduced MC1R function, very sun sensitive, high melanoma risk

description: MC1R variant (V92M) associated with sun sensitivity and melanoma risk, even in individuals without red hair.

sources.name: Valverde P et al. (1995) - Variants of the melanocyte-stimulating hormone receptor gene are associated with red hair and fair skin in humans

notes: MC1R variant (V92M) associated with sun sensitivity and fair skin, though with lower penetrance than classic red-hair MC1R variants. Common across multiple populations. Carriers may have increased UV sensitivity without exhibiting red hair.

TT.description: Each T allele adds ~0.1cm to head circumference on average

CT.description: Intermediate head circumference contribution

CC.description: Neutral or slightly reduced head circumference

description: HMGA2 variant associated with head circumference variation. Same gene region affects overall height. Small individual effect.

sources.name: Ikram MA et al. (2012) - Common variants at 6q22 and 17q21 are associated with intracranial volume

notes: Associated with intracranial volume and head circumference in large GWAS meta-analyses. Effect size is small. Head circumference is highly polygenic and influenced by nutrition and prenatal environment. Not clinically predictive for any individual.

TT.description: Normal weight regulation, lower BMI tendency

AT.description: ~1.5kg higher weight on average, moderately increased obesity risk

AA.description: ~3kg higher weight on average, increased appetite, 1.7x obesity risk

description: FTO gene variant, strongest common genetic predictor of obesity. Each A allele increases weight ~1-1.5kg. Affects appetite regulation and energy expenditure.

sources.name: Frayling TM et al. (2007) - A common variant in the FTO gene is associated with body mass index and predisposes to childhood and adult obesity

notes: Strongest common genetic variant for obesity risk, with ~0.39 kg/m2 BMI increase per risk allele. Effect is consistent across populations but can be attenuated by physical activity. FTO likely acts through regulation of nearby IRX3/IRX5 genes affecting adipocyte thermogenesis.

AA.description: Rapid caffeine breakdown, can drink coffee late without sleep issues, may need more for effects

CC.description: Slow caffeine breakdown, caffeine effects last longer, may have sleep issues with afternoon coffee, increased hypertension risk with high intake

description: CYP1A2*1F variant determining caffeine metabolism speed. AA (fast) metabolizers clear caffeine ~4x faster than CC (slow). Affects coffee tolerance and cardiovascular effects.

sources.name: Sachse C et al. (1999) - Functional significance of a C→A polymorphism in intron 1 of the cytochrome P450 CYP1A2 gene

notes: CYP1A2 variant determining fast vs slow caffeine metabolism. Fast metabolizers (AA genotype) clear caffeine ~4x faster. Clinically relevant — slow metabolizers may have increased cardiovascular risk with high coffee intake. Affects drug metabolism beyond caffeine.

CC.description: Normal cilantro taste - perceives herb flavor

AA.description: Strong soapy/unpleasant taste perception

description: Genetic variation affecting olfactory receptor determining whether cilantro tastes like herbs or soap.

sources.name: Eriksson N et al. (2012) - A genetic variant near olfactory receptor genes influences cilantro preference

notes: Identified in a 23andMe GWAS as associated with perceiving cilantro as soapy-tasting. Located near olfactory receptor gene OR6A2. Effect is moderate — not all carriers dislike cilantro, and cultural exposure can modify preference. Well-replicated finding.

GG.description: Can detect asparagus metabolite odor in urine

AA.description: Cannot detect asparagus urine odor

description: Olfactory receptor variant determining ability to smell asparagus metabolites in urine.

sources.name: Pelchat ML et al. (2011) - Excretion and perception of a characteristic odor in urine after asparagus ingestion: a psychophysical and genetic study

notes: Associated with the ability to detect asparagus metabolite odor in urine. Approximately 40% of people cannot smell the characteristic odor. The trait is harmless and primarily of scientific curiosity. Effect is population-consistent.

CC.description: No photic sneeze reflex

CT.description: Mild sun-induced sneezing

TT.description: Strong sneeze reflex when exposed to bright light

description: Autosomal dominant trait causing sneezing when suddenly exposed to bright light.

sources.name: Eriksson N et al. (2010) - Web-based, participant-driven studies yield novel genetic associations for common traits

notes: Associated with photic sneeze reflex (ACHOO syndrome) in GWAS. The reflex affects ~25% of people and is harmless. Genetic architecture is poorly understood. This SNP explains only a small fraction of the trait.

AA.description: No cheek dimples

AG.description: Likely to have dimples on one or both cheeks

GG.description: Strong tendency for cheek dimples

description: Facial muscle variation creating visible indentations in cheeks when smiling.

sources.name: Shaffer JR et al. (2016) - Genome-wide association study reveals multiple loci influencing normal human facial morphology

notes: Weakly associated with cheek dimples in consumer genetics datasets. Evidence is limited to proprietary GWAS from direct-to-consumer companies; no peer-reviewed replication in independent cohorts. Dimple genetics remain poorly understood.

CC.description: Smooth chin

CT.description: Mild chin cleft

TT.description: Prominent cleft chin

description: Variation in jaw bone fusion creating a visible Y-shaped dimple in the chin.

sources.name: Adhikari K et al. (2016) - A genome-wide association scan in admixed Latin Americans identifies loci influencing facial and scalp hair features

notes: Associated with cleft chin morphology in consumer genetics studies. Limited peer-reviewed evidence. Facial morphology is highly polygenic and influenced by developmental environment. Treat prediction with caution.

GG.description: Straight hairline

AA.description: Distinct widow's peak hairline

description: Characteristic V-shaped point in the center of the hairline.

sources.name: Adhikari K et al. (2016) - A genome-wide association scan in admixed Latin Americans identifies loci influencing facial and scalp hair features

notes: Linked to widow's peak hairline in facial morphology GWAS. The genetic architecture of hairline shape is complex and not well characterized. Limited evidence for this specific SNP-trait association.

AA.description: Cannot roll tongue into tube shape

AG.description: Partial tongue rolling ability

GG.description: Can easily roll tongue into tube shape

description: Ability to curl the lateral edges of the tongue upward into a tube shape.

sources.name: Eriksson N et al. (2010) - Web-based, participant-driven studies yield novel genetic associations for common traits

notes: Associated with tongue rolling ability in consumer genetics platforms. The genetics of tongue rolling have been debated for decades — it is NOT a simple Mendelian trait as commonly taught. Monozygotic twin concordance is only ~70%, indicating environmental influence.

GG.description: Strong morning preference (early bird)

AA.description: Strong evening preference (night owl)

description: Circadian rhythm variation determining natural preference for morning or evening activity.

sources.name: Jones SE et al. (2019) - Genome-wide association analyses of chronotype in 697,828 individuals provides insights into circadian rhythms

notes: Associated with chronotype (morningness/eveningness) in UK Biobank GWAS. Intergenic locus; nearest genes are not definitively established as causal. Effect is modest — circadian preference is influenced by age, light exposure, work schedules, and many other genetic variants. Part of a highly polygenic trait.

GG.description: Normal deep sleep duration

AA.description: Significantly more deep sleep

description: Adenosine deaminase variant affecting deep sleep duration and sleep quality.

sources.name: Retey JV et al. (2005) - A functional genetic variation of adenosine deaminase affects the duration and intensity of deep sleep in humans

notes: ADA variant (Asp8Asn) associated with increased slow-wave (deep) sleep. One of the few replicated single-gene effects on sleep architecture. Carriers of the Asn allele show ~30 minutes more deep sleep. Rare variant (~5-8% in Europeans).

AA.description: Higher pain sensitivity

AG.description: Average pain tolerance

GG.description: Lower pain sensitivity - higher tolerance

description: COMT haplotype variant modulating catecholamine levels and pain perception thresholds.

sources.name: Diatchenko L et al. (2005) - Genetic basis for individual variations in pain perception and the development of a chronic pain condition

notes: COMT haplotype variant influencing pain sensitivity through altered enzyme activity and mRNA structure. Part of a multi-SNP COMT haplotype — individual effect is modest. Pain perception is highly complex with major environmental and psychological components.

GG.description: Low sensitivity to trigger sounds

AA.description: High sensitivity to chewing/repetitive sounds

description: Genetic predisposition to strong emotional reactions triggered by specific sounds like chewing.

sources.name: Sanchez-Roige S et al. (2017) - Genome-wide association studies of impulsive personality traits and misophonia

notes: TENM2 variant linked to misophonia (sound sensitivity) in a single GWAS. Evidence is preliminary — misophonia genetics is poorly understood. The condition likely involves multiple genes and neurological pathways. Replication in larger cohorts is needed.

CC.description: Standard sweet taste perception

CT.description: Slightly enhanced sweet sensitivity

TT.description: Enhanced sweet taste sensitivity - prefers less sugar

description: Sweet taste receptor variant affecting sensitivity to sugars and sweet taste preference.

sources.name: Fushan AA et al. (2009) - Allelic polymorphism within the TAS1R3 promoter is associated with human taste sensitivity to sucrose

notes: TAS1R2 variant influencing sweet taste perception and sugar intake. Effect is moderate — sweet preference is heavily influenced by environment, age, and dietary habits. Some evidence links this variant to differences in sucrose detection thresholds.

CC.description: Separated eyebrows

CT.description: Some eyebrow connectivity

TT.description: Tendency for connected eyebrows (unibrow)

description: Transcription factor variant influencing eyebrow hair growth pattern and density.

sources.name: Adhikari K et al. (2016) - A genome-wide association scan in admixed Latin Americans identifies loci influencing facial and scalp hair features

notes: PAX3 variant associated with unibrow (synophrys) tendency, identified in a Latin American admixed population GWAS. Effect may differ across populations. Eyebrow morphology is influenced by multiple genetic and hormonal factors.

CC.description: Resistant to brain freeze

CT.description: Moderate brain freeze susceptibility

TT.description: Highly susceptible to ice cream headaches

description: Cold-sensing receptor variant affecting susceptibility to cold-stimulus headaches.

sources.name: Anttila V et al. (2013) - Genome-wide meta-analysis identifies new susceptibility loci for migraine

notes: TRPM8 variant primarily associated with migraine susceptibility. The link to 'brain freeze' (cold-stimulus headache) is indirect — TRPM8 is a cold-sensing receptor. Migraine genetics are highly polygenic with hundreds of loci.

CC.description: Low motion sickness risk

CT.description: Moderate motion sickness risk

TT.description: High susceptibility to motion sickness

description: Genetic variation affecting vestibular-visual conflict processing and nausea response.

sources.name: Hromatka BS et al. (2015) - Genetic variants associated with motion sickness point to roles for inner ear development, neurological processes and glucose homeostasis

notes: Identified in a 23andMe GWAS for motion sickness susceptibility. Effect is modest. Motion sickness is influenced by vestibular function, visual processing, and psychological factors. Genetic contribution is polygenic.

TT.description: Less attractive to mosquitoes

CC.description: More attractive to mosquitoes

description: HLA-associated body odor compounds affecting attractiveness to mosquitoes.

sources.name: Fernandez-Grandon GM et al. (2015) - Heritability of attractiveness to mosquitoes

notes: HLA-region variant loosely associated with mosquito bite attraction. Evidence is limited and the mechanism is poorly characterized. Mosquito attraction depends on skin microbiome, CO2 output, body heat, and many non-genetic factors.

GG.description: Standard pitch perception

AA.description: Superior pitch perception ability

description: Vasopressin receptor variant associated with musical aptitude and pitch recognition.

sources.name: Ukkola LT et al. (2009) - Musical aptitude is associated with AVPR1A-haplotypes

notes: AVPR1A variant studied for associations with musical aptitude and social cognition. Evidence is preliminary with small sample sizes. Musical ability is overwhelmingly shaped by training and environment — genetic contributions are minimal and polygenic.

CC.description: Strong right-hand preference

CT.description: Mixed or ambidextrous tendency

TT.description: Left-hand preference more likely

description: Proprotein convertase variant influencing left-right body asymmetry and handedness.

sources.name: Brandler WM et al. (2013) - Common variants in left/right asymmetry genes and pathways are associated with relative hand skill

notes: PCSK6 variant associated with handedness in individuals with dyslexia. Effect on handedness in the general population is very small. Handedness is ~25% heritable with the rest due to prenatal environment and chance. No single gene determines handedness.

GG.description: Lower snoring tendency

TT.description: Higher tendency for snoring

description: Klotho protein variant associated with respiratory muscle tone during sleep.

sources.name: Campos AI et al. (2020) - Genome-wide association study of snoring identifies 42 loci and shared genetic architecture with distinct sleep and respiratory traits

notes: KLOTHO variant (KL-VS) primarily studied for aging and cognitive function. Association with snoring is indirect and based on limited evidence. Snoring is overwhelmingly influenced by BMI, sleeping position, nasal anatomy, and alcohol use.

AA.description: Thinner eyebrows

AG.description: Medium eyebrow thickness

GG.description: Thicker/bushier eyebrows

description: Forkhead transcription factor variant influencing eyebrow hair density and thickness.

sources.name: Adhikari K et al. (2016) - A genome-wide association scan in admixed Latin Americans identifies loci influencing facial and scalp hair features

notes: Associated with eyebrow thickness in admixed Latin American populations. Effect may vary across ethnic groups. Hair morphology traits are polygenic with substantial environmental influence (grooming habits).

AA.description: Higher vitamin D levels

CC.description: Lower vitamin D levels - may need supplementation

description: Vitamin D binding protein variant affecting circulating vitamin D levels.

sources.name: Wang TJ et al. (2010) - Common genetic determinants of vitamin D insufficiency: a genome-wide association study

notes: GC (vitamin D binding protein) variant — one of the strongest genetic determinants of circulating 25(OH)D levels. Clinically relevant: carriers of certain alleles may need higher vitamin D supplementation. Effect is consistent across populations. Vitamin D levels also strongly depend on sun exposure and diet.

CC.description: Lower diabetes risk

CT.description: Moderately increased risk

TT.description: Higher Type 2 diabetes risk

description: Strongest common genetic risk factor for Type 2 diabetes affecting insulin secretion.

sources.name: Grant SFA et al. (2006) - Variant of transcription factor 7-like 2 (TCF7L2) gene confers risk of type 2 diabetes

notes: Strongest common genetic risk factor for type 2 diabetes, with ~1.4x increased risk per T allele. Consistent across all studied populations. However, T2D risk is overwhelmingly driven by lifestyle factors (diet, exercise, weight). Genetic risk can be substantially mitigated by healthy habits.

TT.description: Average longevity tendency

GG.description: Associated with exceptional longevity

description: FOXO3 variant consistently associated with human longevity across multiple populations.

sources.name: Willcox BJ et al. (2008) - FOXO3A genotype is strongly associated with human longevity

notes: FOXO3 variant associated with longevity in multiple populations (European, Japanese, Chinese). G allele carriers show 1.2-1.5x increased odds of reaching 100 years. Mechanism involves improved stress resistance and insulin signaling. Longevity is highly polygenic and environmental.

CC.description: Stronger body odor

CT.description: Moderate body odor

TT.description: Reduced body odor

description: ATP-binding cassette transporter variant affecting apocrine sweat gland secretions.

sources.name: Yoshiura K et al. (2006) - A SNP in the ABCC11 gene is the determinant of human earwax type

notes: Linked to ABCC11 function affecting apocrine gland secretions and body odor intensity. Most relevant in populations polymorphic for ABCC11 variants. The same biological pathway controls earwax type (wet/dry) and axillary odor production.

TT.description: Fewer freckles

GG.description: Dense freckling tendency

description: Interferon regulatory factor variant influencing melanocyte-driven freckle formation.

sources.name: Sulem et al. (2008) - Genetic determinants of hair, eye and skin pigmentation in Europeans

notes: IRF4 regulatory variant associated with freckle density and sun sensitivity in European populations. Pleiotropic effects include contributions to hair color and melanoma risk. Effect size is moderate; freckling is influenced by many loci plus UV exposure.

CC.description: Normal wisdom tooth development

CT.description: Possible missing wisdom teeth

TT.description: Likely missing one or more wisdom teeth

description: Transcription factor variant affecting third molar tooth development and agenesis.

sources.name: Nieminen P et al. (2011) - Identification of new genetic loci associated with dental agenesis

notes: PAX9 variant associated with dental agenesis (missing teeth) including wisdom teeth. Evidence for wisdom tooth development specifically is limited. Dental development is influenced by multiple genes and jaw size/nutrition. Treat predictions with caution.

CC.description: Lower tendon injury risk

CT.description: Average injury risk

TT.description: Higher Achilles tendon injury susceptibility

description: Collagen type V variant affecting tendon flexibility and injury predisposition.

sources.name: Mokone GG et al. (2006) - The COL5A1 gene and Achilles tendon pathology

notes: COL5A1 variant associated with Achilles tendon injury risk in athletes. Effect has been replicated in multiple sports medicine studies. Risk is modifiable through training load management and proper conditioning. Most relevant for competitive athletes.

GG.description: Standard umami perception

AA.description: Highly sensitive to umami/savory taste

description: Taste receptor variant determining sensitivity to glutamate and savory flavors.

sources.name: Shigemura N et al. (2009) - Genetic and molecular basis of individual differences in human umami taste perception

notes: TAS1R1 variant associated with sensitivity to umami (savory) taste. Less well-characterized than bitter taste genetics. Umami perception is influenced by multiple receptor variants and dietary experience.

GG.description: Lower nicotine dependence risk

AA.description: Higher risk of nicotine dependence

description: Nicotinic receptor variant affecting susceptibility to tobacco addiction.

sources.name: Saccone SF et al. (2007) - Cholinergic nicotinic receptor genes implicated in a nicotine dependence association study targeting 348 candidate genes with 3713 SNPs

notes: CHRNA5 variant strongly associated with nicotine dependence and smoking heaviness (~1 extra cigarette/day per risk allele). Also linked to lung cancer risk through increased smoking. One of the most robust addiction genetics findings. Risk allele frequency ~35% in Europeans.

AA.description: Straight hair tendency

TT.description: Curly hair tendency

description: Trichohyalin protein variant affecting hair fiber shape and curliness.

sources.name: Medland SE et al. (2009) - Common variants in the trichohyalin gene are associated with straight hair in Europeans

notes: TCHH variant strongly associated with straight vs curly hair in Europeans. Effect is specific to European populations where the variant is common (~10% frequency). Hair curliness is highly polygenic — this SNP captures only part of the variation.

GG.description: Susceptible to norovirus

AA.description: Resistant to most norovirus strains (non-secretor)

description: Fucosyltransferase variant determining secretor status and norovirus binding.

sources.name: Thorven M et al. (2005) - A homozygous nonsense mutation (428G→A) in the human secretor (FUT2) gene provides resistance to symptomatic norovirus (GGII) infections

notes: FUT2 nonsense variant conferring near-complete resistance to norovirus infection in homozygotes (~20% of Europeans). Non-secretors (AA genotype) lack the gut surface receptor the virus uses for entry. One of the clearest examples of infection resistance genetics.

CC.description: Standard stress cortisol response

GG.description: Enhanced cortisol sensitivity - stronger stress response

description: Glucocorticoid receptor variant affecting hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis sensitivity.

sources.name: DeRijk RH et al. (2006) - A common polymorphism in the mineralocorticoid receptor modulates stress responsiveness

notes: Moderate evidence for NR3C1 variant's role in cortisol stress response. This is one of multiple contributing genetic factors. Environmental and lifestyle factors also play important roles.

TT.description: Free (detached) earlobes

CC.description: Attached earlobes

description: Ectodysplasin receptor variant influencing earlobe attachment pattern.

sources.name: Adhikari K et al. (2015) - A genome-wide association study identifies multiple loci for variation in human ear morphology

notes: EDAR region variant associated with earlobe attachment in multi-ethnic GWAS. Earlobe morphology is polygenic — not the simple dominant/recessive trait described in textbooks. Multiple loci contribute to the continuous spectrum of earlobe attachment.

CC.description: Normal light tolerance

CT.description: Mild light sensitivity

TT.description: Increased light sensitivity (photophobia)

description: NAD(P)H dehydrogenase variant affecting retinal sensitivity to bright light.

sources.name: Siegel D et al. (1999) - NAD(P)H:quinone oxidoreductase 1: role as a superoxide scavenger

notes: NQO1 Pro187Ser variant reducing enzyme activity to near zero. Originally studied for cancer susceptibility. Association with photophobia is indirect and based on limited evidence. NQO1 deficiency affects oxidative stress response in multiple tissues.

CC.description: Lower migraine risk

CT.description: Moderate migraine susceptibility

TT.description: Higher migraine with aura risk

description: Astrocyte glutamate transporter variant affecting cortical excitability and migraine.

sources.name: Anttila V et al. (2010) - Genome-wide association study of migraine implicates a common susceptibility variant on 8q22.1

notes: First GWAS-identified migraine susceptibility locus near MTDH/AEG-1. Effect is modest (OR ~1.2). Migraine with aura has a complex genetic architecture with dozens of confirmed loci. Environmental triggers (stress, sleep, diet) remain the primary modifiable risk factors.

TT.description: Lower SAD risk

CC.description: Higher seasonal affective disorder risk

description: Circadian rhythm gene variant affecting seasonal mood and energy regulation.

sources.name: Levitan RD et al. (2004) - Seasonality-associated serotonin transporter gene polymorphisms and seasonal affective disorder

notes: Tentatively associated with seasonal affective disorder. Evidence is limited — SAD genetics are poorly understood and most studies have been underpowered. The condition is strongly influenced by latitude, light exposure, and serotonin pathway function.

CC.description: Lower bruxism tendency

CT.description: Moderate clenching risk

TT.description: Higher jaw clenching/teeth grinding tendency

description: Serotonin receptor variant associated with sleep bruxism and jaw clenching.

sources.name: Rintakoski K et al. (2012) - Bruxism is associated with nicotine dependence: a nationwide Finnish twin cohort study

notes: HTR2A serotonin receptor variant studied in context of bruxism and various psychiatric conditions. Evidence for bruxism association is preliminary. Serotonin receptor genetics influence many behaviors — single-SNP prediction is unreliable.

GG.description: Typical pitch perception

AA.description: Predisposition for absolute pitch ability

description: HLA region variant associated with rare absolute pitch ability in musicians.

sources.name: Theusch E et al. (2009) - Genome-wide study of families with absolute pitch reveals linkage to 8q24.21 and locus heterogeneity

notes: HLA-A region variant associated with absolute pitch in a linkage study. Evidence is preliminary — absolute pitch has a strong genetic component (~80% heritability) but the specific loci remain poorly characterized. Early musical training is a required environmental cofactor.

CC.description: Standard endurance capacity

CT.description: Moderate endurance advantage

TT.description: Enhanced mitochondrial efficiency for endurance

description: Uncoupling protein variant affecting mitochondrial energy efficiency during prolonged exercise.

sources.name: Dhamrait SS et al. (2004) - Variation in the uncoupling protein 2 and 3 genes and their association with type 2 diabetes and physical performance

notes: UCP2 variant studied for associations with endurance performance and metabolic efficiency. Evidence is mixed across studies. Athletic performance is extremely polygenic with major environmental determinants (training, nutrition). No single variant meaningfully predicts athletic potential.

CC.description: Normal clotting (no Factor V Leiden)

CT.description: Heterozygous Factor V Leiden - slightly increased clot risk

TT.description: Homozygous Factor V Leiden - significantly increased clot risk

description: Factor V Leiden mutation affecting blood clotting cascade and thrombosis risk.

sources.name: Bertina RM et al. (1994) - Mutation in blood coagulation factor V associated with resistance to activated protein C

notes: Factor V Leiden — the most common inherited thrombophilia. Heterozygotes have ~5x increased risk of venous thromboembolism; homozygotes ~50x. Clinically actionable: affects decisions about oral contraceptives, surgery, and prolonged immobilization. Most common in Europeans (~5% carrier rate).

CC.description: Tendency toward extraversion

CT.description: Balanced introversion/extraversion

TT.description: Tendency toward introversion

description: Gene variant associated with personality trait dimensions in large GWAS studies.

sources.name: de Moor MHM et al. (2012) - Meta-analysis of genome-wide association studies for personality

notes: Associated with personality traits in a large GWAS meta-analysis but with very small effect size. Personality is overwhelmingly shaped by environment and experience. Individual genetic variants contribute negligibly to personality prediction. Included for research interest only.

GG.description: Higher baseline aerobic capacity

AA.phenotype: Greater VO2 Max Improvement with Training
AA.description: Greater VO2 max improvement with training

description: PGC-1alpha variant affecting mitochondrial biogenesis and aerobic fitness response.

sources.name: Stefan N et al. (2007) - Genetic variations in PPARD and PPARGC1A determine mitochondrial function and change in aerobic physical fitness and insulin sensitivity during lifestyle intervention

notes: PPARGC1A Gly482Ser variant associated with aerobic fitness response to exercise training. Effect is modest and context-dependent. VO2 max trainability is polygenic — individual genetic variants explain <1% of variation. Training response depends primarily on exercise regimen.

TT.description: Lower celiac/gluten sensitivity risk

TC.phenotype: Carrier for HLA-DQ2 Celiac Risk Allele
TC.description: Carrier for HLA-DQ2 celiac risk allele

CC.description: Higher celiac disease and gluten sensitivity risk

description: HLA-DQ2 haplotype tag SNP strongly associated with celiac disease susceptibility.

sources.name: van Heel DA et al. (2007) - A genome-wide association study for celiac disease identifies risk variants in the region harboring IL2 and IL21

notes: HLA-DQ2.5 haplotype tag SNP — primary genetic risk factor for celiac disease. HLA-DQ2/DQ8 is necessary but not sufficient (~30-40% of the population carries it, but only ~1% develops celiac). Environmental trigger is gluten exposure. Gold standard for celiac genetic risk assessment.

GG.description: Typical fear extinction

AA.description: Stronger fear conditioning and slower extinction

description: Cannabinoid receptor variant affecting amygdala-based fear learning and anxiety.

sources.name: Chakrabarti B et al. (2006) - Variation in the human cannabinoid receptor CNR1 gene modulates gaze duration for happy faces

notes: CNR1 cannabinoid receptor variant studied for associations with anxiety and fear response. Evidence is preliminary with small effect sizes. Fear response is a complex neurobiological phenotype with major environmental and experiential determinants.

GG.description: Normal UV damage recovery

CC.description: Slower sunburn recovery and higher UV sensitivity

description: Melanocortin receptor variant affecting melanin production and UV damage repair.

sources.name: Valverde P et al. (1995) - Variants of the melanocyte-stimulating hormone receptor gene are associated with red hair and fair skin in humans

notes: MC1R variant (D294H) associated with red hair, fair skin, and increased UV sensitivity. Carriers experience slower sunburn recovery and higher melanoma risk. Sun protection is especially important for MC1R variant carriers regardless of apparent hair color.

TT.description: Lower habitual coffee intake

CC.description: Higher habitual coffee consumption tendency

description: Aryl hydrocarbon receptor variant influencing caffeine reward pathway and consumption habits.

sources.name: Cornelis MC et al. (2011) - Genome-wide meta-analysis identifies regions on 7p21 (AHR) and 15q24 (CYP1A2) as determinants of habitual caffeine consumption

notes: AHR variant associated with habitual coffee consumption in large GWAS. AHR regulates CYP1A2 expression, linking this to caffeine metabolism. Effect is consistent across populations. Coffee consumption is also heavily influenced by cultural and socioeconomic factors.

AA.description: Lower baldness risk

CC.description: Higher male pattern baldness risk

description: Histone deacetylase variant associated with androgenetic alopecia susceptibility.

sources.name: Richards JB et al. (2008) - Male-pattern baldness susceptibility locus at 20p11

notes: Associated with androgenetic alopecia at the 20p11 locus. Effect is moderate — male pattern baldness involves multiple loci including AR (X-linked) and this autosomal variant. Prediction accuracy improves substantially with multi-SNP models.

TT.description: Normal alcohol metabolism

TC.description: Faster alcohol to acetaldehyde conversion - mild flush

CC.description: Rapid conversion - strong facial flushing

description: Alcohol dehydrogenase fast-metabolizer variant causing acetaldehyde accumulation.

sources.name: Li H et al. (2009) - Refined geographic distribution of the oriental ALDH2*504Lys (nee 487Lys) variant

notes: ADH1B variant causing rapid alcohol metabolism (Arg48His). Common in East Asian populations (~70-90% frequency). Carriers experience acetaldehyde accumulation causing facial flushing, nausea, and headache with alcohol. Protective against alcoholism but associated with esophageal cancer risk in drinkers.

GG.description: Standard night vision adaptation

AA.description: Better than average night vision

description: Cyclic nucleotide-gated channel variant affecting rod photoreceptor sensitivity.
notes: Limited evidence for common variant effects on scotopic vision. Variant near CNGA1 studied for scotopic (low-light) vision quality. Night vision depends on rod photoreceptor function, pupil dilation, and dark adaptation — highly multifactorial.

CC.description: Lower stretch mark tendency

CT.description: Moderate stretch mark risk

TT.description: Higher stretch mark susceptibility

description: Elastin gene variant affecting skin elasticity and striae formation.

sources.name: Tung JY et al. (2013) - A genome-wide association analysis identifies genetic loci associated with stretch marks

notes: ELN region variant associated with stretch mark (striae distensae) susceptibility in GWAS. Stretch marks are primarily influenced by rapid skin stretching (growth, pregnancy, weight change) and skin elasticity. Genetic contribution is one factor among many.

AA.description: Normal CYP2C9 enzyme activity, standard warfarin dosing appropriate

AC.description: Reduced CYP2C9 activity (~50%), lower warfarin dose may be needed

CC.description: Significantly reduced CYP2C9 activity, substantially lower warfarin dose required

description: CYP2C9*3 variant (I359L) causes significantly reduced metabolism of warfarin and other CYP2C9 substrates. Critical pharmacogenomic marker for anticoagulant dosing.
notes: CYP2C9*3 allele reduces enzyme activity by ~80%. Combined with VKORC1 genotyping, enables pharmacogenomic warfarin dosing. FDA-recommended testing before warfarin initiation.

CC.description: Normal CYP2D6 enzyme activity, standard drug metabolism

CT.description: Reduced CYP2D6 activity, may need dose adjustments for codeine, antidepressants

TT.description: Significantly reduced CYP2D6 activity, codeine ineffective, altered antidepressant response

description: CYP2D6*10 variant causes reduced enzyme activity affecting metabolism of ~25% of all drugs including codeine, tramadol, and many antidepressants.
notes: CYP2D6 metabolizes approximately 25% of clinically used drugs. The *10 allele (P34S) is especially common in East Asian populations (40-70%). Poor metabolizers cannot convert codeine to morphine.

AA.description: No K3326X variant, population-average BRCA2-related cancer risk

AT.description: Carrier of BRCA2 K3326X truncation, modestly increased cancer susceptibility

TT.description: Homozygous BRCA2 K3326X, increased susceptibility to breast and other cancers

description: BRCA2 K3326X is a truncating variant that removes the last 93 amino acids. Associated with moderately increased breast, ovarian, and lung cancer risk.
notes: K3326X is a common BRCA2 truncation (1-2% carrier frequency) with moderate cancer risk. Unlike classic BRCA2 pathogenic variants, the risk increase is more modest (~2-fold for breast cancer). Genetic counseling is recommended for carriers.

CC.phenotype: No FAP Risk from This Variant
CC.description: Wild-type APC gene at this position, no increased polyposis risk

CT.description: Heterozygous APC mutation carrier, high risk of colorectal adenomatous polyps and cancer

TT.description: Homozygous APC mutation, very high risk of early-onset colorectal cancer

description: APC tumor suppressor gene mutation causing familial adenomatous polyposis (FAP), characterized by hundreds to thousands of colorectal adenomas with near-certain cancer progression if untreated.
notes: APC mutations are responsible for FAP, an autosomal dominant condition with near 100% penetrance for colorectal cancer by age 40-50 without intervention. Prophylactic colectomy is standard of care. Genetic counseling is recommended for carriers.

CC.description: Normal CYP2C9*1 activity, standard warfarin dosing

CT.description: Intermediate CYP2C9 activity (~60%), may require lower warfarin dose

TT.description: Poor CYP2C9 activity (~30%), significantly lower warfarin dose needed

description: CYP2C9*2 variant (R144C) reduces warfarin metabolism. Combined with CYP2C9*3 and VKORC1 status, guides pharmacogenomic anticoagulant dosing.
notes: CYP2C9*2 reduces enzyme activity by ~30-40%. Common in European populations (10-15%). Together with *3 and VKORC1 genotype, accounts for ~40% of warfarin dose variability.

CC.description: Wild-type BRCA1 at this position, population-average cancer risk

CT.description: BRCA1 variant carrier, significantly increased lifetime breast and ovarian cancer risk

TT.description: Homozygous BRCA1 variant, very high cancer susceptibility

description: BRCA1 missense variant associated with increased susceptibility to hereditary breast and ovarian cancer syndrome. BRCA1 is a critical tumor suppressor involved in DNA double-strand break repair.
notes: BRCA1 pathogenic variants confer 60-80% lifetime breast cancer risk and 20-40% ovarian cancer risk. Genetic counseling and enhanced screening recommended for carriers.

GG.description: Normal COL1A1 expression, average bone density and fracture risk

GT.description: Increased COL1A1 transcription, slightly reduced bone mineral density and increased fracture risk

TT.description: Significantly increased COL1A1 expression, lower bone density and higher fracture susceptibility

description: COL1A1 Sp1 binding site polymorphism affects collagen type I alpha-1 chain expression, influencing bone mineral density and osteoporotic fracture risk.
notes: The T allele increases Sp1 transcription factor binding, leading to overproduction of the alpha-1 chain relative to alpha-2, producing weaker bone collagen. Effect is most pronounced in postmenopausal women.

CC.description: Full MTHFR enzyme activity, normal folate and homocysteine metabolism

CT.description: ~65% MTHFR enzyme activity, mildly elevated homocysteine levels

TT.description: ~30% MTHFR enzyme activity, elevated homocysteine, increased folate supplementation needs

description: MTHFR C677T (Ala222Val) reduces methylenetetrahydrofolate reductase activity, impairing folate metabolism and raising homocysteine levels. One of the most clinically relevant nutrigenomic variants.
notes: TT genotype has ~30% residual enzyme activity. Elevated homocysteine is a cardiovascular risk factor. Adequate folate intake can compensate. Important during pregnancy for neural tube defect prevention.

CC.description: Full alpha-actinin-3 expression in fast-twitch muscle fibers, favors power and sprint performance

CT.description: Reduced alpha-actinin-3, balanced power and endurance capacity

TT.description: Complete alpha-actinin-3 deficiency (R577X), muscle fibers shift toward endurance characteristics

description: ACTN3 R577X determines presence of alpha-actinin-3 in fast-twitch muscle fibers. The most replicated sports genetics variant, influencing power vs endurance athletic capacity.
notes: The 577X (T) allele creates a premature stop codon, eliminating alpha-actinin-3. ~18% of humans worldwide are XX (TT). Nearly absent in elite sprinters but enriched in endurance athletes.

CC.description: Standard VDR protein length, normal vitamin D signaling and bone metabolism

CT.description: Heterozygous VDR FokI, intermediate vitamin D receptor activity

TT.description: Extended VDR protein with altered activity, may affect bone mineral density and calcium absorption

description: VDR FokI polymorphism affects the start codon of the vitamin D receptor, producing proteins of different lengths with varying transcriptional activity. Influences bone mineral density and calcium metabolism.
notes: FokI polymorphism is unique among VDR variants as it actually changes the protein structure (3 amino acids shorter). The shorter protein (f allele, C) has slightly higher transcriptional activity.

AG.description: One non-functional CYP2D6 allele, reduced metabolism of CYP2D6 substrates

AA.description: Non-functional CYP2D6 (*4/*4), unable to metabolize many common drugs including codeine, tamoxifen

GG.description: Normal CYP2D6 enzyme function, standard drug metabolism

description: CYP2D6*4 is the most common non-functional allele in Europeans, causing a splicing defect that eliminates enzyme activity. Affects metabolism of ~25% of prescription drugs.
notes: CYP2D6*4 (G>A at intron 3/exon 4 boundary) is the most common PM allele in Europeans (20-25%). Homozygotes are poor metabolizers (~7% of Europeans). FDA recommends CYP2D6 testing for multiple drugs.

AA.description: Non-functional CYP2C19 (*2/*2), clopidogrel ineffective, high risk of cardiovascular events on standard therapy

AG.description: Reduced CYP2C19 activity, diminished clopidogrel activation, consider alternative antiplatelet therapy

GG.description: Full CYP2C19 activity, normal clopidogrel activation and antiplatelet response

description: CYP2C19*2 is the most common loss-of-function allele, preventing activation of the prodrug clopidogrel. FDA black box warning mandates genotyping consideration before clopidogrel use.
notes: CYP2C19*2 aberrant splice site eliminates enzyme activity. 15-30% of most populations carry at least one *2 allele. FDA black box warning on clopidogrel labels regarding CYP2C19 poor metabolizers.

CC.description: Two copies of e4 allele, 8-15x increased Alzheimer's risk, earlier onset of cognitive decline

CT.description: One copy of e4 allele, 3-4x increased Alzheimer's risk, potential earlier cognitive decline

TT.phenotype: No e4 Allele at This Position
TT.description: No e4 allele from this SNP, baseline Alzheimer's risk (must consider rs7412 for full APOE haplotype)

description: APOE rs429358 defines the e4 allele (with rs7412). APOE e4 is the strongest common genetic risk factor for late-onset Alzheimer's disease, affecting amyloid-beta clearance in the brain.
notes: APOE haplotype requires both rs429358 and rs7412: e2=T/T, e3=T/C, e4=C/C at these two loci. e4 homozygotes have ~50% Alzheimer's risk by age 85. Most impactful common genetic variant for dementia.

CC.phenotype: No e2 Allele at This Position
CC.description: Standard APOE at this position, baseline risk (must consider rs429358 for full haplotype)

CT.description: One copy of protective e2 allele, ~40% reduced Alzheimer's risk

TT.description: Two copies of protective e2 allele, significantly reduced Alzheimer's risk

description: APOE rs7412 defines the e2 allele (with rs429358). APOE e2 is protective against Alzheimer's disease, associated with more efficient amyloid-beta clearance.
notes: APOE e2 (rs429358-T + rs7412-T) reduces Alzheimer's risk by ~40-50% per allele. However, e2/e2 homozygotes have increased risk of type III hyperlipoproteinemia. ~7% of population carries at least one e2.

CC.description: Wild-type BRCA2 at this position, population-average cancer risk

CT.description: BRCA2 pathogenic variant carrier, significantly increased breast, ovarian, and other cancer risks

TT.description: Homozygous BRCA2 pathogenic variant, very high cancer susceptibility

description: BRCA2 pathogenic variant disrupting DNA double-strand break repair via homologous recombination. Carriers have substantially elevated lifetime breast, ovarian, prostate, and pancreatic cancer risk.
notes: BRCA2 pathogenic variants confer ~45-55% lifetime breast cancer risk and ~15-25% ovarian cancer risk. Also increases male breast cancer, prostate, and pancreatic cancer risk. PARP inhibitors are effective targeted therapy. Genetic counseling is recommended for carriers.

CC.description: Low VKORC1 sensitivity, requires higher warfarin doses (5-7 mg/day typical)

CT.description: Moderate VKORC1 sensitivity, intermediate warfarin dose requirement (3-5 mg/day typical)

TT.description: High VKORC1 sensitivity, requires lower warfarin doses (1.5-3 mg/day typical), higher bleeding risk at standard doses

description: VKORC1 -1639G>A promoter variant is the single strongest predictor of warfarin dose requirement, accounting for ~25% of dose variability. Affects vitamin K epoxide reductase expression.
notes: VKORC1 genotype is the strongest single predictor of warfarin dose. The T allele reduces VKORC1 expression, making patients more sensitive to warfarin. Very common in East Asian populations (>90% TT).

CC.description: Proline variant, stronger cell cycle arrest and DNA repair, slower apoptosis

CG.description: Heterozygous, balanced apoptosis and repair capacity

GG.description: Arginine variant, more efficient apoptosis, potentially better tumor suppression but faster aging

description: TP53 codon 72 polymorphism (Arg72Pro) affects p53 protein function, balancing apoptotic efficiency versus DNA repair capacity. Modifies cancer susceptibility and aging-related phenotypes.
notes: Arg72 has 15x stronger apoptotic activity than Pro72, but Pro72 is better at cell cycle arrest and repair. Population frequencies vary by latitude — Pro72 is more common near the equator.

GG.description: Both risk alleles for exfoliation syndrome and secondary glaucoma

TT.description: Protective genotype, reduced exfoliation syndrome risk

description: LOXL1 R141L variant is strongly associated with pseudoexfoliation syndrome, the most common identifiable cause of open-angle glaucoma worldwide.
notes: LOXL1 encodes a lysyl oxidase-like protein essential for elastic fiber homeostasis. The risk allele frequency varies dramatically across populations, and paradoxically the risk allele flips between populations.

GG.description: Wild-type ARMS2, population-average age-related macular degeneration risk

GT.description: Heterozygous ARMS2 risk variant, ~2.5x increased AMD risk

TT.description: Homozygous ARMS2 risk variant, ~8x increased risk of age-related macular degeneration

description: ARMS2 A69S variant is the second strongest genetic risk factor for age-related macular degeneration, the leading cause of blindness in the elderly.
notes: ARMS2/HTRA1 locus is the second major AMD risk locus after CFH. Risk allele frequency ~20% in Europeans, ~40% in East Asians. Homozygous carriers have ~8x AMD risk.

CC.description: Homozygous CFH Y402H risk variant, ~6x increased AMD risk

CT.description: Heterozygous CFH variant, ~2.5x increased AMD risk

TT.description: Wild-type CFH, population-average macular degeneration risk

description: CFH Y402H is the strongest genetic risk factor for age-related macular degeneration. Complement factor H regulates the alternative complement pathway in the retina.
notes: CFH Y402H was one of the first GWAS discoveries. Risk allele frequency ~35% in Europeans. The variant impairs complement regulation in the retina, leading to chronic inflammation and drusen formation.

AA.description: Non-risk genotype at 9p21 locus, baseline myocardial infarction risk

AG.description: One risk allele, ~25% increased heart attack risk independent of traditional risk factors

GG.description: Two risk alleles, ~50% increased heart attack risk independent of cholesterol and blood pressure

description: 9p21.3 locus variant near CDKN2A/2B is the strongest common genetic risk factor for myocardial infarction, acting independently of traditional cardiovascular risk factors.
notes: 9p21 risk is independent of LDL cholesterol, blood pressure, diabetes, and smoking. The mechanism involves regulation of CDKN2A/2B (cell cycle control) and ANRIL long non-coding RNA. Risk allele frequency ~50%.

AA.description: Full TPMT enzyme activity, standard azathioprine/6-MP dosing appropriate

AG.description: Reduced TPMT activity, dose reduction recommended to prevent myelosuppression

GG.description: Very low TPMT activity, high risk of life-threatening myelosuppression at standard doses

description: TPMT*3C variant reduces thiopurine methyltransferase activity. Critical for dosing azathioprine, 6-mercaptopurine, and thioguanine to prevent potentially fatal bone marrow toxicity.
notes: TPMT deficiency affects ~0.3% of population (homozygous) and ~10% are intermediate metabolizers. FDA mandates TPMT testing before thiopurine therapy. TPMT*3C is the most common variant in East Asian and African populations.

CC.description: Standard CYP2C19 metabolizer status, normal drug activation and metabolism

CT.description: Increased CYP2C19 activity, faster metabolism of PPIs and clopidogrel activation

TT.description: Significantly increased CYP2C19 activity, may need higher PPI doses, enhanced clopidogrel response

description: CYP2C19*17 promoter variant increases gene expression, producing ultra-rapid metabolism of proton pump inhibitors, antidepressants, and enhanced clopidogrel activation.
notes: *17 carriers metabolize PPIs too quickly, reducing efficacy. For clopidogrel, *17 enhances activation (beneficial). Frequency ~20-25% in Europeans, 3-5% in East Asians.

CC.description: Homozygous risk genotype, ~30-60% increased coronary artery disease risk

CG.description: Heterozygous, ~15-30% increased coronary artery disease risk

GG.description: Non-risk genotype at 9p21 locus, population-average coronary artery disease risk

description: 9p21.3 variant near CDKN2A/2B is one of the most replicated coronary artery disease risk loci, affecting vascular cell proliferation and atherosclerotic plaque stability.
notes: This 9p21 locus was discovered simultaneously by multiple GWAS groups in 2007. Risk allele frequency ~50%. Effect is independent of traditional cardiovascular risk factors.

CC.description: Normal GDF5 expression, baseline osteoarthritis risk

CT.description: Reduced GDF5 expression, moderately increased knee and hip osteoarthritis risk

TT.description: Significantly reduced GDF5 expression, higher susceptibility to osteoarthritis

description: GDF5 +104T/C promoter polymorphism reduces expression of growth differentiation factor 5, a key regulator of joint development and cartilage maintenance.
notes: GDF5 is essential for joint and cartilage development. The T allele reduces promoter activity by ~20%. Risk allele is very common (>50% in most populations). Also associated with height variation.

CC.description: Higher FADS1 activity, efficient conversion of plant-based omega-3 (ALA) to EPA/DHA

CT.description: Moderate FADS1 activity, intermediate omega-3/omega-6 conversion efficiency

TT.description: Lower FADS1 activity, less efficient omega-3 conversion, may benefit more from direct fish oil supplementation

description: FADS1 fatty acid desaturase 1 variant affects the conversion efficiency of plant-based omega-3 (ALA) to EPA and DHA, and omega-6 (LA) to arachidonic acid.
notes: FADS1/FADS2 cluster variants show strong signals of recent natural selection. The efficient-desaturation allele was selected for in populations with plant-based diets. Affects blood lipid profiles.

AA.description: Homozygous CHEK2 I157T, substantially increased breast, colon, and prostate cancer risk

AG.description: CHEK2 I157T carrier, ~1.5x increased breast cancer risk, modest increase in other cancers

GG.description: Wild-type CHEK2, population-average cancer susceptibility

description: CHEK2 I157T missense variant impairs checkpoint kinase 2 function in DNA damage response. Moderate-penetrance breast cancer susceptibility allele also linked to colorectal and prostate cancer.
notes: CHEK2 I157T is a moderate-penetrance cancer allele. More common in Northern Europeans (~5%). Risk is lower than BRCA1/2 but higher than common GWAS hits. Also increases colorectal and prostate cancer risk. Genetic counseling is recommended for carriers.

AA.description: Lower ACE activity, improved endurance performance, efficient oxygen utilization

AG.description: Intermediate ACE levels, mixed endurance and power capacity

GG.description: Higher ACE activity, increased angiotensin II, favors power and strength performance

description: ACE insertion/deletion polymorphism affects circulating and tissue ACE levels. The I allele (lower ACE) favors endurance, while the D allele (higher ACE) favors power and strength.
notes: ACE I/D is one of the most studied sports genetics variants. D allele frequency ~55% globally. Effect sizes are modest but consistent across studies. Also relevant to hypertension risk and ACE inhibitor response.

CC.description: Full XRCC1 R194W activity, efficient base excision repair of DNA damage

CT.description: Intermediate XRCC1 activity, slightly reduced base excision repair efficiency

TT.description: Lower XRCC1 efficiency, impaired base excision repair, modestly increased cancer susceptibility

description: XRCC1 R194W variant affects base excision repair (BER) pathway efficiency. XRCC1 scaffolds multiple BER enzymes and its variants modify susceptibility to DNA damage-induced cancers.
notes: XRCC1 R194W (Arg194Trp) is more common in East Asian populations (20-30%) than Europeans (5-10%). The variant allele may actually be protective in some contexts, with study results being mixed. Genetic counseling is recommended for carriers.

AA.description: Homozygous prothrombin mutation, significantly elevated blood clot risk

AG.description: Heterozygous prothrombin G20210A, ~3x increased venous thromboembolism risk

GG.description: Wild-type prothrombin, population-average thrombosis risk

description: Factor II (prothrombin) G20210A is the second most common inherited thrombophilia after Factor V Leiden. Increases prothrombin levels, elevating blood clot risk.
notes: G20210A is a gain-of-function variant in the 3' UTR that increases prothrombin mRNA stability and protein levels. Carrier frequency ~2% in Europeans. Important for contraceptive and surgical risk assessment.

CC.description: Normal thiopurine methyltransferase function, standard dosing appropriate

CT.description: Reduced TPMT enzyme stability, dose reduction recommended for thiopurines

TT.description: Severely reduced TPMT activity, high risk of life-threatening toxicity at standard thiopurine doses

description: TPMT*3B component (Y240C) causes enzyme instability and rapid degradation. Part of the TPMT*3A haplotype when combined with *3C, the most common deficient allele in Europeans.
notes: TPMT*3A (combination of *3B and *3C) is the most common deficiency allele in Europeans (~5%). The Y240C substitution causes misfolding and rapid proteasomal degradation of the enzyme.

AA.description: H1/H1 MAPT haplotype, slightly increased risk of progressive supranuclear palsy and tauopathies

AG.description: Mixed MAPT haplotype, intermediate tauopathy risk

GG.description: H2/H2 MAPT haplotype, reduced risk of tauopathies and Parkinson's disease

description: MAPT H1/H2 haplotype tag SNP affects tau protein expression and splicing. H1 is associated with increased risk of tauopathies including progressive supranuclear palsy and Parkinson's disease.
notes: The H1 haplotype spans a 1.8Mb inversion on chromosome 17. H2 frequency ~20% in Europeans but very rare in East Asian and African populations. H1 increases tau expression and 4-repeat tau isoforms.

AA.description: Homozygous HFE C282Y, high risk of iron overload and hemochromatosis requiring monitoring/treatment

AG.description: HFE C282Y carrier, mildly elevated iron stores but typically asymptomatic

GG.description: Wild-type HFE, normal iron absorption and regulation

description: HFE C282Y is the primary cause of hereditary hemochromatosis type 1, causing excessive iron absorption that can damage the liver, heart, and pancreas if untreated.
notes: C282Y homozygosity is present in ~0.5% of Northern Europeans. Penetrance is incomplete (~28% of male and ~1% of female homozygotes develop clinical hemochromatosis). Regular phlebotomy prevents complications.

AA.description: Reduced MLH1 promoter activity, increased risk of colorectal, endometrial, and other Lynch syndrome cancers

AG.description: Moderately reduced MLH1 expression, somewhat elevated mismatch repair-related cancer risk

GG.description: Full MLH1 promoter activity, normal DNA mismatch repair capacity

description: MLH1 promoter variant affects expression of a key DNA mismatch repair gene. Reduced MLH1 expression increases microsatellite instability and Lynch syndrome-associated cancer risk.
notes: MLH1 -93G>A promoter variant reduces transcription factor binding. The A allele is associated with MLH1 promoter methylation and microsatellite instability in tumors. Frequency ~25% in Europeans. Genetic counseling is recommended for carriers.

AA.description: Full enzyme activity at A1298C position, normal folate metabolism

AC.description: Slightly reduced MTHFR activity (~85%), mild effect on folate metabolism

CC.description: Reduced MTHFR activity (~60-70%), may compound with C677T for elevated homocysteine

description: MTHFR A1298C (Glu429Ala) variant mildly reduces enzyme activity. Clinically significant mainly when compound heterozygous with C677T, impairing folate-mediated one-carbon metabolism.

sources.name: van der Put NM et al. (1998)

notes: A1298C alone has mild effect. Compound heterozygosity (677CT + 1298AC) can reduce MTHFR activity to ~50%, similar to 677TT alone. The C allele frequency is ~30% in most populations.

AA.description: Impaired XRCC1 function, reduced base excision repair, modestly increased cancer susceptibility

AG.description: Heterozygous Arg399Gln, slightly reduced repair capacity

GG.description: Full XRCC1 Arg399 function, efficient base excision repair

description: XRCC1 Arg399Gln affects the BRCT1 domain critical for protein-protein interactions in base excision repair. The most studied XRCC1 polymorphism for cancer susceptibility.
notes: Arg399Gln is in the BRCT1 domain that interacts with PARP1 and poly(ADP-ribose). The Gln allele reduces repair efficiency after oxidative damage. Gln allele frequency ~35% in Europeans, ~25% in East Asians. Genetic counseling is recommended for carriers.

GG.description: LA/LA genotype, higher serotonin transporter expression, potentially greater stress resilience

GT.description: Mixed alleles, intermediate serotonin transporter expression and stress response

TT.description: LG/LG genotype, lower serotonin transporter expression, similar to short allele carriers in stress sensitivity

description: SLC6A4 rs25531 subdivides the long (L) allele of the serotonin transporter promoter into LA (high expression) and LG (low expression, functionally similar to the short allele).
notes: This SNP refines the classic 5-HTTLPR serotonin transporter polymorphism. The LG allele has expression similar to the S allele. Gene-environment interaction with stressful life events is debated. Note: The 5-HTTLPR gene-environment interaction failed to replicate in Border et al. 2019 meta-analysis (N=443,264). Individual SNP effects on stress resilience are modest.

AC.description: Heterozygous TERT variant, intermediate telomere maintenance capacity

AA.description: Lower TERT expression, tendency toward shorter telomeres and accelerated cellular aging

CC.description: Higher TERT expression, better telomere maintenance, associated with both longevity and increased cancer risk

description: TERT (telomerase reverse transcriptase) variant affects telomere length maintenance. Longer telomeres are associated with longevity but paradoxically also with increased cancer risk.
notes: TERT is the catalytic subunit of telomerase. The C allele associates with longer telomeres but also increased lung cancer, glioma, and bladder cancer risk — illustrating the aging-cancer tradeoff.

AA.description: Lower melanin production, lighter skin pigmentation

AG.description: Moderate melanin, intermediate skin tone

GG.description: Higher melanin production, darker skin pigmentation

description: SLC45A2 L374F variant strongly associated with skin pigmentation differences across populations.
